subject: computer_science
title: System Change Management
stem_template: An organisation is replacing its legacy {{system}} with a new cloud-based solution. Which implementation strategy installs the new system alongside the old one, running them simultaneously for a period?
answer_template: Parallel running — both old and new systems operate simultaneously; outputs are compared to verify the new system; safest approach but most expensive and resource-intensive.
distractor_templates: Direct changeover (big bang) — immediately switching off the old system and activating the new one on a set date.
Pilot changeover — implementing the new system in one department/location first before rolling out company-wide.
Phased changeover — gradually replacing modules of the old system one at a time over time.

options_template.text: Parallel running — both systems run simultaneously for comparison and verification.
Direct changeover — immediate switch from old to new on a set date.
Pilot changeover — new system tested in one department first.
Phased changeover — old system replaced module by module over time.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for parallel running + simultaneous operation + comparison of outputs.
hints: Parallel running is the safest but most costly method.
Direct changeover is riskiest — if the new system fails, there is no fallback.

subject: computer_science
title: User Training for New Systems
stem_template: When introducing a new {{system}} to {{n_users}} users, which approach to training is most effective for reducing resistance to change?
answer_template: A combination of hands-on training, clear documentation, and involving key users ('champions') early in the process helps reduce resistance; involving end-users in system design and testing (user acceptance testing) builds ownership and reduces anxiety.
distractor_templates: Forcing all users to use the new system immediately with no training minimises transition costs.
Only training the IT department is sufficient — they can help other users on an ad-hoc basis.
Training is unnecessary if the new system is sufficiently intuitive.

options_template.text: Hands-on training, documentation, early user involvement (champions), and user acceptance testing.
Force immediate adoption with no training to minimise transition costs.
Train only the IT department; they support others ad-hoc.
No training needed if system is sufficiently intuitive.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for any two valid training/change management strategies with correct reasoning.
hints: User involvement reduces resistance to change (change management theory).
Documentation, tutorials, and 'super users' are all valid strategies.

subject: computer_science
title: System Development Life Cycle (SDLC) Phases
stem_template: Arrange the following phases of the System Development Life Cycle (SDLC) in the correct order.
answer_template: 1. Feasibility Study — assess technical, financial, and legal viability. 2. Requirements Analysis — gather and define user/system requirements. 3. System Design — design architecture, interfaces, and data structures. 4. Development (Coding) — build the system according to design specifications. 5. Testing — verify the system meets requirements (unit, integration, acceptance testing). 6. Implementation — deploy the system (parallel/pilot/phased/direct changeover). 7. Maintenance and Review — ongoing support, bug fixes, and updates.
mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark per correctly placed adjacent phase (max 6).
hints: The SDLC is iterative in modern development (Agile) but sequential in waterfall.
Feasibility always comes first; maintenance is the ongoing final phase.

subject: computer_science
title: Impact of a New System on an Organisation
stem_template: A school is replacing its manual attendance tracking system with an automated {{system}}. Analyse the impact on three stakeholder groups and identify two potential risks of the implementation.
answer_template: Stakeholders: (1) Students — faster check-in, but privacy concerns about biometric/location data. (2) Teachers — reduced administrative burden; require training on new system. (3) IT staff — responsible for maintenance and security; increased workload initially. Risks: (1) Data security/privacy breach (personal data of students stored digitally — GDPR compliance). (2) Technical failure during implementation (system downtime disrupts attendance tracking). Mitigation: pilot testing, data encryption, staff training, backup procedures.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark per stakeholder impact (max 3); 1 mark per risk with explanation (max 2); 1 mark mitigation. Max 6.
hints: Consider privacy (GDPR), training, and technical reliability.
Stakeholder analysis: who gains, who is inconvenienced, who bears risk?

subject: computer_science
title: System Inputs, Processes, and Outputs
stem_template: In an automated {{system}}, the {{input}} is the system input, the {{process}} is the processing step, and the {{output}} is the output. Which additional element creates a closed-loop control system?
answer_template: Feedback — the output is fed back into the system as a new input, allowing the system to self-regulate and adjust behaviour to maintain desired conditions (e.g. thermostat measures actual temperature again after heater activates).
distractor_templates: Storage — data is stored for future reference but does not affect system behaviour.
Interface — the user interface through which inputs are entered manually.
Control — a human operator who monitors outputs and manually adjusts inputs.

options_template.text: Feedback — output becomes input; system self-regulates to maintain target state.
Storage — data stored for future reference; doesn't affect behaviour.
Interface — user enters inputs manually.
Control — human operator adjusts inputs based on outputs.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for feedback + self-regulation/closed-loop description.
hints: Closed-loop system: feedback loop allows automatic correction.
Open-loop system: no feedback; output does not influence input.

subject: computer_science
title: Data Flow Diagrams
stem_template: In a Data Flow Diagram (DFD), which symbol represents an external entity (source or destination of data outside the system)?
answer_template: A rectangle (square) — represents external entities such as customers, suppliers, or other systems that interact with the system but are outside its boundary.
distractor_templates: A circle or rounded rectangle — this represents a process (transformation of data).
An open rectangle (two parallel lines) — this represents a data store.
An arrow — this represents data flow between components.

options_template.text: Rectangle — external entity outside system boundary.
Circle/rounded rectangle — process (data transformation).
Open rectangle (two parallel lines) — data store.
Arrow — data flow direction.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for rectangle + external entity definition.
hints: DFD symbols: Rectangle = external entity; Circle = process; Open rectangle = data store; Arrow = data flow.
External entities are outside the system boundary.

subject: computer_science
title: System Design — Inputs, Processes, Outputs, Feedback
stem_template: Design the IPO (Input-Process-Output) model for a {{system}}. Include at least two inputs, two processes, two outputs, and describe the feedback mechanism.
answer_template: Library system: Inputs: book ID (barcode scan), student ID, return date. Processes: check availability in database, calculate due date, update loan records. Outputs: loan confirmation message, overdue notices, inventory reports. Feedback: overdue notices trigger system to flag account; return scanning updates availability status, which feeds back into the availability checking process. Each element accurately described and linked to the system's function.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark per input (max 2); 1 mark per process (max 2); 1 mark per output (max 2); 1 mark feedback mechanism. Max 7, cap at 6.
hints: IPO model: what goes in, what happens to it, what comes out.
Feedback closes the loop — output becomes a new input or triggers a system response.

subject: computer_science
title: CPU Components and Functions
stem_template: The {{component}} of the CPU is responsible for:
answer_template: ALU: performs arithmetic (add, subtract, multiply) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, comparisons) operations. CU: fetches, decodes, and coordinates execution of instructions; generates control signals. PC: holds the memory address of the next instruction to be fetched. Accumulator: temporarily stores the result of the most recent ALU operation.
distractor_templates: Permanently storing program instructions and data between sessions.
Controlling input/output devices through hardware interrupts only.
Encrypting data before it is sent to main memory.

options_template.text: ALU: arithmetic/logical operations. CU: fetch-decode-execute. PC: address of next instruction. Accumulator: stores ALU result.
All components: permanently store programs between sessions.
All components: control I/O through hardware interrupts.
All components: encrypt data before sending to memory.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark per correct component-function match (max 4).
hints: The CU orchestrates the fetch-decode-execute cycle.
The PC is automatically incremented after each fetch.

subject: computer_science
title: Logic Gates — AND, OR, NOT
stem_template: Complete the truth table for the logic expression: \\( Y = (A \\text{ AND } B) \\text{ OR } (\\text{NOT } A) \\)
answer_template: A=0,B=0: NOT A=1, A AND B=0, Y=1. A=0,B=1: NOT A=1, A AND B=0, Y=1. A=1,B=0: NOT A=0, A AND B=0, Y=0. A=1,B=1: NOT A=0, A AND B=1, Y=1.
mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark per correct row (max 4). Penalise if intermediate columns omitted but correct final column still awarded.
hints: Work out NOT A first, then A AND B, then combine with OR.
NOT flips the bit: NOT 0 = 1, NOT 1 = 0.

subject: computer_science
title: NAND and NOR Logic Gates
stem_template: Complete the truth table for: (a) NAND gate: \\( Y = \\overline{A \\cdot B} \\), (b) NOR gate: \\( Y = \\overline{A + B} \\) for all four input combinations.
mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark per correct NAND row (max 4); 1 mark per correct NOR row (max 4). Max 8, capped at 4 marks.
hints: NAND is AND followed by NOT. Only output 0 when both inputs are 1.
NOR is OR followed by NOT. Only output 1 when both inputs are 0.

subject: computer_science
title: Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle
stem_template: Describe the fetch-decode-execute cycle for the instruction stored at memory address {{address}}. Name the registers involved at each stage.
answer_template: 1. FETCH: Program Counter (PC) holds address {{address}}; address is copied to Memory Address Register (MAR); data at {{address}} is fetched into Memory Data Register (MDR); instruction copied to Current Instruction Register (CIR); PC incremented to point to next instruction. 2. DECODE: Control Unit (CU) decodes the opcode in CIR to determine the type of instruction. 3. EXECUTE: CU activates appropriate components — ALU for arithmetic/logic, or other registers for data movement; result stored in Accumulator.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark per stage (fetch/decode/execute) with correct register (max 3); 1 mark per register correctly named and explained (max 3). Max 6.
hints: Key registers: PC, MAR, MDR, CIR, Accumulator.
PC is incremented during the fetch stage, before execution.

subject: computer_science
title: Primary vs Secondary Memory
stem_template: Which of the following correctly distinguishes RAM from ROM?
answer_template: RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile (loses data when power is off), read/write, used for temporary storage of currently running programs and data. ROM (Read Only Memory) is non-volatile, read-only, contains permanent firmware (e.g. BIOS).
distractor_templates: RAM is non-volatile and stores permanent firmware; ROM is volatile and used for temporary program storage.
Both RAM and ROM are volatile; they differ only in access speed.
ROM has faster access time than RAM because it uses solid-state technology.

options_template.text: RAM: volatile, read/write, temporary. ROM: non-volatile, read-only, permanent firmware.
RAM: non-volatile, permanent firmware. ROM: volatile, temporary.
Both volatile; differ only in speed.
ROM is faster than RAM due to solid-state design.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for volatile/non-volatile distinction AND read/write vs read-only distinction.
hints: RAM = Random Access Memory (volatile). ROM = Read Only Memory (non-volatile).
BIOS/UEFI firmware is stored in ROM.

subject: computer_science
title: SSD vs HDD
stem_template: A user is choosing between an SSD and an HDD for secondary storage. Which statement correctly describes an advantage of SSDs over HDDs?
answer_template: SSDs have no moving mechanical parts (flash memory) — faster read/write speeds, lower latency, more durable (shock-resistant), quieter, and lower power consumption compared to HDDs.
distractor_templates: HDDs are faster because spinning platters allow simultaneous multi-track access.
SSDs are slower because they must verify each write operation for data integrity.
HDDs have no moving parts, making them more reliable than SSDs.

options_template.text: SSDs: no moving parts → faster, more durable, quieter, lower power.
HDDs are faster due to simultaneous multi-track spinning access.
SSDs are slower due to write verification overhead.
HDDs have no moving parts — more reliable than SSDs.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for no moving parts + at least one advantage (speed, durability, or power).
hints: SSD = solid-state drive (flash memory). HDD = hard disk drive (spinning platters).
SSDs are faster but more expensive per GB; HDDs are cheaper per GB.

subject: computer_science
title: Memory Hierarchy and Trade-offs
stem_template: Explain the memory hierarchy from registers to cloud storage, describing the trade-off between speed, capacity, and cost at each level. Give an example of data stored at each level.
answer_template: 1. Registers (CPU): fastest, smallest (bytes), most expensive per bit; holds current instruction operands. 2. Cache (L1/L2/L3): very fast, small (KB–MB), expensive; stores frequently accessed data to reduce RAM access. 3. RAM: fast, medium capacity (GB), moderate cost; holds running programs and OS. 4. SSD/HDD (Secondary): slower, large capacity (TB), cheap; permanent program/file storage. 5. Cloud/archival storage: slowest access (network), virtually unlimited, lowest cost per GB; backups, large datasets. Trade-off: speed ↑ → cost ↑ → capacity ↓.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark per level correctly described with speed/capacity/cost (max 5); 1 mark trade-off principle. Max 6.
hints: The closer to the CPU, the faster and more expensive the memory.
Cache bridges the speed gap between registers and RAM.

subject: computer_science
title: LAN vs WAN
stem_template: Which of the following correctly distinguishes a Local Area Network (LAN) from a Wide Area Network (WAN)?
answer_template: LAN: confined to a small geographic area (building/campus), owned and controlled by the organisation, high speed (typically 100Mbps–10Gbps). WAN: spans large geographic areas (cities/countries/globally), uses third-party infrastructure (ISPs), lower speeds relative to LAN.
distractor_templates: LANs always use wireless technology; WANs always use wired connections.
WANs are faster than LANs because they use fibre optic connections throughout.
LANs require an internet connection; WANs are always private networks.

options_template.text: LAN: small area, organisation-owned, high speed. WAN: large area, third-party infrastructure, global reach.
LANs = wireless; WANs = wired.
WANs are faster than LANs (fibre optic).
LANs require internet; WANs are private.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for geographic scope distinction AND ownership/infrastructure distinction.
hints: LAN = Local (within building). WAN = Wide (across cities/countries).
The internet is a global WAN; your school network is a LAN.

subject: computer_science
title: Network Topologies
stem_template: In a {{topology}} network topology, all devices are connected to a central {{hub_switch}}. What is a key disadvantage of this topology?

hub_switch.formula: topology === 'star' ? 'hub or switch' : topology === 'bus' ? 'backbone cable' : 'ring cable'

answer_template: Star topology: if the central switch/hub fails, the entire network goes down (single point of failure). Bus topology: if the backbone cable fails, all devices lose connectivity. Ring topology: if any node or cable fails, the entire network is disrupted.
distractor_templates: Star: requires the most cable of any topology — highest infrastructure cost.
Star: data collisions are most frequent because all devices share one cable.
Star: devices cannot communicate directly — all data must travel through every other device.

options_template.text: Star: central switch failure takes down entire network (single point of failure).
Star: requires the most cabling — highest cost.
Star: data collisions most frequent due to shared cable.
Star: data must travel through every device — slow.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for correct disadvantage of the given topology (single point of failure for star; backbone failure for bus).
hints: Star: failure of central switch = entire network down.
Bus: backbone failure = network down. Ring: any failure = network disrupted.

subject: computer_science
title: OSI Model Layers
stem_template: Describe the function of each layer of the OSI model (7 layers) and give one example of a protocol or technology at each layer.
answer_template: 1. Physical: transmits raw bits over physical medium (e.g. Ethernet cables, Wi-Fi signals). 2. Data Link: frames data, handles MAC addressing, error detection (e.g. Ethernet, Wi-Fi 802.11). 3. Network: logical addressing and routing (e.g. IP protocol). 4. Transport: end-to-end communication, reliability, flow control (e.g. TCP, UDP). 5. Session: establishes, maintains, terminates communication sessions (e.g. NetBIOS). 6. Presentation: data formatting, encryption, compression (e.g. SSL/TLS, JPEG). 7. Application: interface for user applications (e.g. HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP).
mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark per layer with correct function (max 7). Cap at 6 marks. Accept equivalent examples.
hints: Mnemonic: 'Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away' (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
TCP/IP model merges some OSI layers: Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access.

subject: computer_science
title: Packet Switching
stem_template: In packet-switched networks, data is divided into packets. Which statement correctly describes how packets reach their destination?
answer_template: Packets may take different routes through the network; they are reassembled in the correct order at the destination using sequence numbers; this provides resilience (if one route fails, packets use another).
distractor_templates: All packets must follow the same pre-defined route from source to destination (circuit switching).
Packets are always delivered in the same order they were sent without reassembly.
Packets are broadcast to every device on the network; only the destination accepts them.

options_template.text: Packets may take different routes; reassembled at destination using sequence numbers; resilient to failures.
All packets follow the same pre-defined route (circuit switching).
Packets delivered in exact send order without reassembly.
Packets broadcast to all devices; destination accepts them.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for different routes possible + reassembly at destination. Bonus for resilience/error-checking.
hints: Packet switching: each packet independently routed; reassembled at destination.
Circuit switching: dedicated path maintained throughout (e.g. telephone calls).

subject: computer_science
title: Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption
stem_template: HTTPS uses a hybrid encryption approach. In the initial handshake, {{asym_key}} encryption is used to securely exchange a {{sym_key}} key, which is then used for the session. Why is the {{sym_key}} key used for the bulk of data transmission?
answer_template: Symmetric encryption is much faster than asymmetric encryption for bulk data; once the symmetric key is securely exchanged using asymmetric encryption, all subsequent data is encrypted/decrypted with the faster symmetric key.
distractor_templates: Symmetric encryption is more secure than asymmetric, so it must be used for sensitive data.
Asymmetric encryption cannot handle large amounts of data due to key length limitations.
The symmetric key is used because it does not require any encryption — it is sent in plaintext.

options_template.text: Symmetric encryption is faster for bulk data; asymmetric used only for key exchange.
Symmetric is more secure than asymmetric — used for sensitive data.
Asymmetric cannot handle large data due to key length.
Symmetric key sent in plaintext — no encryption needed.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for speed advantage of symmetric encryption + asymmetric used only for key exchange.
hints: Asymmetric = public/private key pair (RSA, ECC) — slow but secure key exchange.
Symmetric = shared secret key (AES) — fast for bulk data.

subject: computer_science
title: Data Transmission — Error Detection
stem_template: Explain how parity checking is used to detect errors in data transmission. A byte transmitted with even parity is {{byte}}. Has an error occurred? Show your working.
answer_template: Even parity: count of 1-bits in the byte must be even. For {{byte}}: count the 1s → if odd count, error detected; if even, no error detected. Parity checking: before transmission, a parity bit is added to make total 1s even (even parity) or odd (odd parity). Limitation: can detect single-bit errors but not double-bit errors (two errors cancel out); cannot correct errors (only detects them). For error correction, use more sophisticated methods (e.g. checksums, CRC, Hamming code).
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark even parity definition; 1 mark correctly counting 1s in {{byte}}; 1 mark correct error/no-error conclusion; 1 mark limitation of parity. Max 4.
hints: Even parity: count all 1-bits including the parity bit — should total to an even number.
Parity only detects odd numbers of bit errors.

subject: computer_science
title: Abstraction in Computational Thinking
stem_template: A programmer is designing a navigation app. Instead of modelling every physical detail of roads (texture, colour, material), they model only the {{relevant_features}}. This is an example of:

values: traffic light timings and pedestrian crossings

answer_template: Abstraction — removing unnecessary detail and representing only the information relevant to solving the problem; allows complex systems to be modelled and managed at an appropriate level of detail.
distractor_templates: Decomposition — breaking a complex problem into smaller, manageable sub-problems.
Pattern recognition — identifying recurring patterns or regularities in data.
Algorithm design — creating a step-by-step procedure to solve the problem.

options_template.text: Abstraction — removing irrelevant detail, keeping only relevant information.
Decomposition — breaking into smaller sub-problems.
Pattern recognition — identifying recurring regularities.
Algorithm design — step-by-step solution procedure.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for abstraction + removing unnecessary detail / focusing on relevant features.
hints: Abstraction: 'what' not 'how'; simplifies complex reality.
A map is an abstraction of geographical reality.

subject: computer_science
title: Big-O Time Complexity
stem_template: A linear search algorithm checks each element in a list of {{n}} items one at a time until the target is found. In the worst case, what is its time complexity?
answer_template: \\( O(n) \\) — in the worst case, the algorithm must check all n elements; as the list size doubles, the maximum number of comparisons doubles (linear relationship).
distractor_templates: \\( O(1) \\) — constant time; the algorithm always finds the element in one step.
\\( O(n^2) \\) — quadratic; the algorithm compares every pair of elements.
\\( O(\\log n) \\) — logarithmic; as in binary search.

options_template.text: \\( O(n) \\) — worst case: check all n elements (linear).
\\( O(1) \\) — constant time.
\\( O(n^2) \\) — quadratic time.
\\( O(\\log n) \\) — logarithmic (binary search).

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for O(n) with correct explanation (worst case: n comparisons for n items).
hints: O(n): linear; O(log n): binary search; O(n²): bubble sort worst case.
Big-O describes growth rate of time/space as n increases.

subject: computer_science
title: Bubble Sort Algorithm Steps
stem_template: Arrange the steps of one complete pass of the bubble sort algorithm for the array [{{a}}, {{b}}, {{c}}, {{d}}].
answer_template: 1. Compare first pair ({{a}}, {{b}}): if {{a}} > {{b}}, swap → [{{b}}, {{a}}, {{c}}, {{d}}]. 2. Compare next pair (position 2,3): if out of order, swap. 3. Compare next pair (position 3,4): if out of order, swap. 4. After one pass, the largest element has 'bubbled' to the last position. 5. Repeat for n-1 passes (excluding sorted elements at end each time).
mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark per correctly sequenced step. Max 4.
hints: Bubble sort: repeatedly compare adjacent pairs and swap if out of order.
Each pass places the next-largest element in its correct position.

subject: computer_science
title: Binary Search Trace
stem_template: Trace a binary search for the value {{target}} in the sorted array: [{{arr}}]. Show each step including the low, high, and mid pointers.
answer_template: Step 1: low=0, high=n-1, mid=⌊(low+high)/2⌋. Check arr[mid] vs {{target}}. If equal → found. If arr[mid] < {{target}} → low = mid+1. If arr[mid] > {{target}} → high = mid-1. Repeat until found or low > high (not found). Binary search requires a sorted array; time complexity = O(log n) vs O(n) for linear search.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark per step correctly shown with low/mid/high values (max 4); 1 mark comparison logic; 1 mark O(log n) complexity. Max 6.
hints: Binary search halves the search space each iteration.
O(log₂ n) — for 1024 elements, at most 10 comparisons needed.

subject: computer_science
title: Flowchart Symbols
stem_template: In a flowchart, which symbol is used to represent a decision (conditional branch) such as an IF statement?
answer_template: A diamond (rhombus) — represents a decision point with two or more exits (usually Yes/No or True/False), corresponding to conditional branching (IF...THEN...ELSE).
distractor_templates: A rectangle — represents a process or action step.
An oval/rounded rectangle — represents start and end points (terminators).
A parallelogram — represents input/output operations.

options_template.text: Diamond/rhombus — decision point with Yes/No exits.
Rectangle — process or action step.
Oval/rounded rectangle — start/end (terminator).
Parallelogram — input/output operation.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for diamond + decision/conditional branch description.
hints: Flowchart shapes: Terminal=oval, Process=rectangle, Decision=diamond, I/O=parallelogram.
Decision symbol has exactly two exits: Yes and No.

subject: computer_science
title: Pseudocode Trace Table
stem_template:
Trace the following pseudocode and complete a trace table showing the value of each variable for each iteration:

```
count ← 0
sum ← 0
loop while count < {{n}}
  count ← count + 1
  sum ← sum + count
end loop
output sum
```
answer_template: For n={{n}}: Iteration 1: count=1, sum=1. Iteration 2: count=2, sum=3. Iteration 3: count=3, sum=6. Iteration 4: count=4, sum=10. [Continues to n={{n}}] Final output: sum = {{n}}*({{n}}+1)/2 = {{result}}. This computes the sum of integers from 1 to {{n}}.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark per correct row in trace table (max n marks); 1 mark correct final output {{result}}. Cap at 5 marks.
hints: Track count and sum at each iteration.
The loop runs while count < n, so it executes n times.

subject: computer_science
title: Algorithm Design — Pseudocode
stem_template: Write pseudocode for an algorithm that reads {{n}} numbers from the user, calculates their {{operation}}, and outputs the result. Include input validation (reject negative numbers).
answer_template:
```
total ← 0
count ← 0
loop for i ← 1 to {{n}}
  input number
  if number >= 0 then
    total ← total + number
    count ← count + 1
  else
    output 'Invalid input: number must be non-negative'
  end if
end loop
if count > 0 then
  result ← total / count  // for mean
  output result
else
  output 'No valid numbers entered'
end if
```
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark loop structure; 1 mark input validation (reject negatives); 1 mark accumulation logic; 1 mark correct operation (mean/max/min); 1 mark output with error handling. Max 5.
hints: Include: loop, input, validation, accumulation, calculation, output.
Consider edge cases: all inputs invalid (count = 0).

subject: computer_science
title: Variables and Data Types
stem_template: A programmer declares a variable to store the number of students in a class ({{n}} students). Which data type is most appropriate?
answer_template: Integer (int) — the number of students is a whole number (no fractional parts); integer data type is most memory-efficient and semantically correct for counting.
distractor_templates: Float/real — required for any numerical data in programming.
String — because the number will be displayed on screen.
Boolean — because a class either has students or does not.

options_template.text: Integer (int) — whole number, counting, no fractional parts.
Float/real — required for all numerical data.
String — because it will be displayed on screen.
Boolean — class either has or doesn't have students.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for integer + correct reasoning (whole number/counting).
hints: Integer: whole numbers. Float: decimal numbers. String: text. Boolean: true/false.
Use the most restrictive (specific) data type appropriate to the data.

subject: computer_science
title: FOR vs WHILE Loops
stem_template: A program needs to repeat a block of code exactly {{n}} times. Which loop structure is most appropriate?
answer_template: A FOR loop — used when the number of iterations is known in advance (exactly {{n}} times); more concise and clear than a while loop for this purpose.
distractor_templates: A WHILE loop — always preferred because it is more flexible.
A DO...WHILE loop — because it guarantees at least one execution.
A REPEAT...UNTIL loop — always used when iterating a fixed number of times.

options_template.text: FOR loop — number of iterations known in advance ({{n}} times); clearest structure.
WHILE loop — always preferred for flexibility.
DO...WHILE — guarantees at least one execution.
REPEAT...UNTIL — always used for fixed iterations.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for FOR loop + correct reason (known number of iterations).
hints: FOR loop: use when iteration count is known. WHILE loop: use when condition-driven.
DO...WHILE/REPEAT...UNTIL: always executes at least once (condition checked at end).

subject: computer_science
title: Array Operations — Pseudocode
stem_template: Write pseudocode to find the largest element in an array of {{n}} integers named numbers[]. Trace your algorithm with the example: [{{arr}}].
answer_template:
```
max ← numbers[0]
for i ← 1 to n-1
  if numbers[i] > max then
    max ← numbers[i]
  end if
end for
output max
```
Trace for [{{arr}}]: max initialised to {{first}}; iterate comparing each element; final max = {{maximum}}.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark initialise max to first element; 1 mark loop from index 1; 1 mark correct comparison; 1 mark update max; 1 mark correct trace with final answer. Max 5.
hints: Initialise max to first element (not 0, which may not be in the array).
Iterate from index 1 (not 0) since index 0 is already stored in max.

subject: computer_science
title: Stack Data Structure — LIFO
stem_template: A stack uses Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) ordering. If elements are pushed in order: {{e1}}, {{e2}}, {{e3}}, {{e4}}, then two elements are popped, what remains on the stack?
answer_template: After pushing {{e1}}, {{e2}}, {{e3}}, {{e4}} (top = {{e4}}), two pops remove {{e4}} then {{e3}}. Remaining stack (bottom to top): {{e1}}, {{e2}}. Top of stack = {{e2}}.
distractor_templates: {{e1}} and {{e4}} remain — FIFO removes from the front.
The stack is empty — pop removes all elements added.
{{e3}} and {{e4}} remain — LIFO pushes newer elements to the bottom.

options_template.text: {{e1}} and {{e2}} remain ({{e4}} and {{e3}} popped first — LIFO). Top = {{e2}}.
{{e1}} and {{e4}} remain — FIFO removes from front.
Stack is empty — pop removes all elements.
{{e3}} and {{e4}} remain — newer elements pushed to bottom.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for correct identification of popped elements ({{e4}}, {{e3}}) + remaining elements ({{e1}}, {{e2}}).
hints: Stack = LIFO: last element pushed is the first to be popped.
Think of a stack of plates — you always take from the top.

subject: computer_science
title: Binary Tree — Traversal Orders
stem_template: For a binary tree with root {{root}}, left child {{left}}, and right child {{right}}, what is the in-order traversal result?
answer_template: In-order traversal (Left → Root → Right): {{left}}, {{root}}, {{right}}. Pre-order (Root→L→R): {{root}}, {{left}}, {{right}}. Post-order (L→R→Root): {{left}}, {{right}}, {{root}}.
distractor_templates: {{root}}, {{left}}, {{right}} — this is pre-order (root first).
{{left}}, {{right}}, {{root}} — this is post-order.
{{right}}, {{root}}, {{left}} — this is reverse in-order.

options_template.text: In-order: {{left}}, {{root}}, {{right}} (Left→Root→Right).
{{root}}, {{left}}, {{right}} — pre-order traversal.
{{left}}, {{right}}, {{root}} — post-order traversal.
{{right}}, {{root}}, {{left}} — reverse in-order.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for correct in-order sequence {{left}}, {{root}}, {{right}}.
hints: In-order: L → Root → R (gives sorted order for a BST).
Pre-order: Root → L → R; Post-order: L → R → Root.

subject: computer_science
title: Linked List Operations
stem_template: A singly linked list contains nodes with values: {{n1}} → {{n2}} → {{n3}} → {{n4}} → null. Describe how to: (a) insert a new node with value {{new_val}} after the node with value {{n2}}, and (b) delete the node with value {{n3}}.
answer_template: (a) INSERT {{new_val}} after {{n2}}: 1. Traverse to node {{n2}}. 2. Create new node with value {{new_val}}. 3. Set new_node.next = node({{n2}}).next (currently points to {{n3}}). 4. Set node({{n2}}).next = new_node. Result: {{n1}}→{{n2}}→{{new_val}}→{{n3}}→{{n4}}. (b) DELETE {{n3}}: 1. Traverse to node before {{n3}} (which is {{new_val}} after insertion). 2. Set prev_node.next = node({{n3}}).next = {{n4}}. 3. Free/dispose of node({{n3}}). Result: {{n1}}→{{n2}}→{{new_val}}→{{n4}}.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark traversal to correct position; 1 mark pointer update (insert); 1 mark new node creation; 1 mark deletion pointer update; 1 mark memory deallocation. Max 5.
hints: Insertion: update pointers before inserting to avoid losing reference to rest of list.
Deletion: bypass the node by linking predecessor directly to successor.

subject: computer_science
title: Binary Search Tree — Insertion Order
stem_template: Insert the following values into an empty binary search tree (BST) in the given order: {{v1}}, {{v2}}, {{v3}}, {{v4}}, {{v5}}. Arrange the resulting tree structure showing parent-child relationships.
answer_template: 1. Insert {{v1}} → root. 2. Insert {{v2}} < {{v1}} → left child of root. 3. Insert {{v3}} > {{v1}} → right child of root. 4. Insert {{v4}} < {{v1}}, < {{v2}} → left child of {{v2}}. 5. Insert {{v5}} > {{v1}}, < {{v3}} → left child of {{v3}}. BST property: left subtree values < parent; right subtree values > parent.
mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark per correct node placement relative to its parent (max 5). Must show BST property maintained.
hints: BST: insert left if new value < current node; insert right if greater.
Each insertion starts from the root and traverses down.

subject: computer_science
title: Recursion — Base Case
stem_template: A recursive function to calculate the factorial of n is defined. What is the base case that prevents infinite recursion?
answer_template: factorial(0) = 1 (or factorial(1) = 1) — when n reaches 0 or 1, the function returns 1 without making a further recursive call, stopping the recursion.
distractor_templates: factorial(n) = n × factorial(n+1) — this is the recursive case, not the base case.
factorial(n) = 0 when n is negative — this handles errors but is not the base case.
The function never needs a base case if sufficient memory is available for the call stack.

options_template.text: factorial(0) = 1 — returns without recursive call; stops recursion.
factorial(n) = n × factorial(n+1) — recursive case (not base).
factorial(n) = 0 when n is negative — error handling, not base case.
No base case needed if sufficient call stack memory.

mark_scheme_template: Award 1 mark for n=0 (or n=1) returns 1 + explanation that no further recursive call is made.
hints: Base case: the simplest case that can be solved directly without recursion.
Without a base case → infinite recursion → stack overflow error.

subject: computer_science
title: Recursive Function Trace
stem_template:
Trace the execution of the following recursive function for the call fibonacci({{n}}):

```
function fibonacci(n)
  if n <= 1 then
    return n
  else
    return fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)
  end if
end function
```
Show the call stack and return values.
answer_template: fibonacci({{n}}) calls fibonacci({{n}}-1) + fibonacci({{n}}-2). Each call branches further until base cases (n=0 returns 0, n=1 returns 1). The call tree has 2^n calls in worst case → O(2^n) time complexity. fibonacci(4)=3, fibonacci(5)=5, fibonacci(6)=8. Stack depth = n. This demonstrates why iterative or memoised implementations are more efficient.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark base cases (n≤1 returns n); 1 mark per level of call trace (max 3); 1 mark correct final result {{result}}; 1 mark exponential time complexity noted. Max 6.
hints: Draw the call tree — each non-base call branches into two sub-calls.
fibonacci(n) result: fibonacci(4)=3, (5)=5, (6)=8.

subject: computer_science
title: Recursion vs Iteration — Comparison
stem_template: Compare recursive and iterative implementations of {{algorithm}}. Write pseudocode for each and analyse trade-offs in terms of efficiency, readability, and memory use.
answer_template: Recursive {{algorithm}}: elegant, closely mirrors mathematical definition, easier to understand for complex problems (e.g. tree traversal). Disadvantages: each call adds a stack frame → O(n) memory for call stack; risk of stack overflow for large n; may have redundant calculations (Fibonacci without memoisation = O(2^n)). Iterative equivalent: O(1) space (constant memory, uses loop variables); generally faster due to no function call overhead; less elegant for some problems. Conclusion: recursion preferred for clarity in naturally recursive problems (trees, divide-and-conquer); iteration preferred for performance-critical, deep recursion cases.
mark_scheme_template: Award: 1 mark recursive pseudocode; 1 mark iterative pseudocode; 1 mark memory trade-off; 1 mark efficiency comparison; 1 mark appropriate conclusion. Max 5.
hints: Recursive: clean code but stack overhead. Iterative: efficient but less readable for recursive problems.
Tail-call optimisation can make recursion as efficient as iteration in some languages.

subject: computer_science
title: Data Structure Properties
stem_template: Which of the following are TRUE about a STACK data structure? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: Elements are added and removed from the same end
It follows Last In, First Out (LIFO) ordering
It is used in breadth-first search (BFS)
The main operations are push and pop
It follows First In, First Out (FIFO) ordering

mark_scheme_template: Stack: LIFO; push/pop from same end (top). NOT FIFO (that's a queue); NOT used in BFS (BFS uses a queue; DFS uses a stack).
hints: Stack = LIFO (like a stack of plates).
Queue = FIFO; Stack = LIFO.

subject: computer_science
title: Algorithm Complexity
stem_template: Which of the following statements about algorithm time complexity are TRUE? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: O(1) means constant time regardless of input size
A bubble sort has worst-case complexity O(n²)
O(log n) is slower than O(n) for large inputs
Binary search has O(log n) time complexity
O(n!) algorithms scale well to large inputs

mark_scheme_template: TRUE: O(1) constant; bubble sort O(n²); binary search O(log n). FALSE: O(log n) is FASTER than O(n); O(n!) is extremely slow (factorial growth — terrible for large n).
hints: Order: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2^n) < O(n!)
Faster = fewer operations for large n.

subject: computer_science
title: Network Protocols
stem_template: Which of the following are TRUE about the TCP/IP model? Select ALL that apply.

options_template.text: TCP provides reliable, ordered data delivery
UDP is more reliable than TCP
IP is responsible for addressing and routing packets
HTTP operates at the application layer
TCP performs handshaking before data transfer

mark_scheme_template: TCP: reliable, ordered, handshaked (3-way handshake). IP: addressing/routing. HTTP: application layer. FALSE: UDP is faster but LESS reliable (no error checking/retransmission).
hints: TCP = reliable, slow; UDP = fast, unreliable.
IP handles routing, TCP handles data integrity.

subject: computer_science
title: Data Structures to Use Cases
stem_template: Match each data structure to its most appropriate use case.
right: Printer job management (first come, first served)
mark_scheme_template: Stack → Undo/redo; Queue → Printer jobs (FIFO); Hash Table → fast lookup; Binary Tree → hierarchical structure.
hints: Stack: LIFO = undo/redo.
Queue: FIFO = first in, first served.

subject: computer_science
title: Binary to Decimal Conversion
stem_template: Convert the 8-bit binary number \({{b7}}{{b6}}{{b5}}{{b4}}{{b3}}{{b2}}{{b1}}{{b0}}\) to its decimal value.

steps_template.label: Identify place values
steps_template.content: Binary positional values (right to left): 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128. Multiply each bit by its place value.

mark_scheme_template: Decimal value = \({{b7}} \times 128 + {{b6}} \times 64 + {{b5}} \times 32 + {{b4}} \times 16 + {{b3}} \times 8 + {{b2}} \times 4 + {{b1}} \times 2 + {{b0}} \times 1 = {{answer}}\)
hints: Work from left to right: bit 7 = 128, bit 0 = 1.
Only add place values where the bit is 1.
